verses.text: The LORD to Moses: command Israel — my offering, my bread for my sacrifices made by fire, of a sweet savour, ye shall observe to offer in due season. Guidance: God speaks as if He had a table, a kitchen, a household. My bread — said of a God who needs nothing. He calls Israel's offerings 'food' not because He is hungry, but because He wants to be close enough to share a meal. Worship is dinner at His table, on His clock.

The LORD to Moses: command Israel — my offering, my bread for my sacrifices made by fire, of a sweet savour, ye shall observe to offer in due season. Guidance: God speaks as if He had a table, a kitchen, a household. My bread — said of a God who needs nothing. He calls Israel's offerings 'food' not because He is hungry, but because He wants to be close enough to share a meal. Worship is dinner at His table, on His clock.

Daily offering: two lambs without spot — one in the morning, one at even — with a tenth of flour mingled with oil, and a quarter of a hin of strong wine. A continual burnt offering, ordained in mount Sinai, for a sweet savour to the LORD. Guidance: A lamb at sunrise and a lamb at sundown — Israel's day bracketed by sacrifice. Worship was not a Sunday event; it was breakfast and dinner. The continual fire on the altar said: God is always being remembered, and always remembering His people.

On the sabbath day: two lambs of the first year without spot, two tenths of flour for a meat offering, and the drink offering — beside the continual burnt offering and his drink offering. Guidance: The sabbath doesn't replace the daily — it doubles it. Rest in God's economy is not a pause from worship; it is more worship piled on more. The day off has its own altar, fuller than the working day's.

The day off has its own altar, fuller than the working day's.

In the beginnings of your months: two young bullocks, one ram, seven lambs without spot — with their meat and drink offerings. One kid of the goats for a sin offering. Beside the continual burnt offering and his drink offering. Guidance: Every new moon, Israel started the month at the altar. Time itself sanctified at its turning. A sin offering at every monthly threshold says: we begin each fresh page already needing grace. Holiness is not a destination one moves past — it is the constant beginning.

The fourteenth day is the LORD's Passover. The fifteenth begins the feast — seven days of unleavened bread. First day, a holy convocation, no servile work. Offer two bullocks, one ram, seven lambs with their meat and drink, and one goat for a sin offering, daily for seven days. Seventh day also a holy convocation. Guidance: The week of Passover scaled the daily offerings sevenfold. Memory of deliverance was not a single dinner — it was a whole week of fire on the altar. The story of the Lamb was told until it sank in.

On the day of firstfruits, when ye bring a new meat offering after your weeks be out, ye shall have a holy convocation, no servile work. Offer two bullocks, one ram, seven lambs — with their meat and drink offerings — and one kid of the goats for a sin offering. Guidance: Fifty days after Passover comes the wheat harvest — Pentecost. The first grain of the new crop returns to God's table. The pattern is steady: lambs, flour, oil, wine, a sin offering. Worship is not improvised — it is the rhythm that keeps the harvest grateful.
